message.content:
Adversaries may exploit Microsoft Management Console (MMC) to execute malicious .msc files, leveraging its ability to manage system configurations and administrative tools. MMC, a Microsoft-signed binary, can open and save custom consoles containing snap-ins for local or remote system management. Attackers can use MMC commands to perform harmful actions, such as deleting backup catalogs without user prompts, or execute malicious .msc files by creating a registry Class Identifier (CLSID) subkey linked to a custom console. This technique allows adversaries to proxy execution of malicious payloads, potentially leading to system compromise and data loss.

Common exploitation methods include creating and invoking malicious .msc files, manipulating registry keys, and using MMC commands to bypass security measures. The impact can range from unauthorized system changes to complete system recovery inhibition.

Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and restricting the use of MMC and .msc files, implementing strict access controls, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious MMC activities. Regular audits of system configurations and registry keys can also help identify and mitigate potential abuses of this technique.
